design_philosophy.core_loop: Navigate ship around circular perimeter, destroy incoming enemy waves with converging fire, progress through planetary systems via warps with bonus stages between bosses
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Balancing peripheral awareness (enemies spawn from screen edges and center) with precise circular movement and convergent aiming mechanics
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Commanding a lone space defense force vessel (Gyruss) traveling through the solar system to overthrow a tyrannical dictator; progression framed as approaching home (Earth) after banishment
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Tube shooter perspective creates unique constraint: player confined to circular perimeter while all shots converge to center—forces tactical positioning over reflexive dodging. NES version adds boss encounters and super-phaser mechanic, making it more boss-driven progression fantasy than arcade's pure wave-clearing

difficulty.lives_system: Extra lives awarded at score thresholds (capped at 999,990 points); Konami code can grant additional lives at title screen in NES version
difficulty.checkpointing: Bonus rounds after each planet completion; no mid-stage checkpoints
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Score-based life gain creates natural difficulty scaling; weapon pickups (satellites, smart bomb, phasers) provide temporary power spikes that alter challenge curve locally

progression.upgrade_system: Satellite pickups provide: double guns (standard), super phasers (energy-based alternative weapon), smart bomb, extra lives. Energy management for phaser attacks adds resource layer absent in arcade version
progression.unlock_mechanism: Linear progression through planetary sequence: Neptune (2 warps) → Uranus (3 warps) → Saturn → Jupiter → Mars → Earth (each requiring 3 warps after first), then repeat with increased difficulty. NES version includes Pluto, Venus, Mercury, and Sun as additional stages (39 total vs arcade's 24)
replayability_hooks: Score chasing with bonus stages incentivizing perfect enemy clears (40 enemies = 10,000 points vs 100 each)
Weapon pickup timing decisions (when to use phasers vs conserve energy)
Konami code meta-unlock for extra lives

world_building_techniques: Opening text scroll (FDS only): establishes 26th-century setting, authoritarian Federal Government, protagonist Rudolf as exiled rebel leader returning after 10-year sentence
Planetary journey framing: each warp toward/away from planets creates spatial narrative of homecoming
Ending sequence (FDS full, NES abbreviated): shows ship journey to Sun and back, text affirms 'Universe at Peace' after defeating dictator Domesu (Rudolf's father)
Regional localization shifts: Japanese version emphasizes political context (dark age, revolution, regime change); US version minimizes narrative, focusing on 'hero saves universe' abstraction

boss_design.philosophy: NES enhancement: boss encounters added at each planetary arrival to punctuate progression and provide narrative climax moments. Contrasts arcade's pure enemy-wave elimination with boss-focused stage structure
notable_bosses: Planet bosses at Neptune, Uranus, Saturn, Jupiter, Mars, Earth, and Sun (specific patterns not documented in sources)
Final boss: Domesu (the dictatorial father figure, defeated to achieve ending)

danger_signals: Authoritarian military monitoring (mentioned in Japanese intro)
Dictatorial control over entire solar system
environmental_storytelling: One-point perspective tube shooter creates claustrophobic, forward-momentum aesthetic (traveling *through* space toward threats)
Planetary sequence geography: Neptune to Earth journey mirrors returning home against galactic opposition
Bonus stages present as respite moments, framing bosses as mandatory conflicts within exploration

sources_used: tcrf - The Cutting Room Floor: Gyruss (NES, Famicom Disk System)
